column7: description
initial state — 190.51 tok/s with Qwen3-0.6B-4bit
remove redundant mx.eval on cache states during prefill — cache eval is required for materialization
overlap Metal compute with Python yield — noise, confirmation run showed 168 tok/s
keep y as (1,1) shape — no measurable improvement, within noise
mx.compile decode step — severe regression, likely retracing due to mutable cache
switch KV cache to concatenation — simpler code, marginal improvement
use mx.async_eval to pipeline Metal compute with Python — median ~201 tok/s
async_eval for prefill — regression, prefill needs sync to materialize cache before next chunk
only compute lm_head on last token during generation — saves prefill compute
pre-allocated KV buffers — slower than concat for these sizes
batch RoPE for Q and K — massive regression from concat/split overhead
call mx.fast SDPA directly — unexpected severe regression
increase bench runs to 5 and warmup to 64 for stability
single forward pass for small prompts — cleaner, within noise
set 4GB Metal cache limit — slight improvement, good practice
2-step graph batching — major improvement, ~225 tok/s median
N=4 step graph batching — 234 tok/s, +23% from baseline
N=8 step graph batching — 242 tok/s, +27% from baseline
N=16 step graph batching — 247 tok/s, +30% from baseline
N=32 — no improvement over N=16, diminishing returns
squeeze vs slice — no difference
skip redundant slice for single-token decode — cleaner logic
inline sampling — no improvement, worse readability
N=12 — worse than N=16
expose eval_batch_size parameter — API improvement, no perf change
wired limit — no improvement
